rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the source text: policy_statement states "Build ultra-low energy buildings" and verbatim_text repeats "BUILD ULTRA-LOW ENERGY BUILDINGS." This directly implies reducing operational energy demand in buildings (energy efficiency/resource efficiency), matching the canonical mechanism "ultra_low_energy_new_buildings → energy_use_reduction." The gate failure ('no Resource Efficiency outcome token found') appears to be a keyword/tokenization miss rather than lack of climate relevance. climate_screen is 'explicit_self', supporting keep.
mechanism_description: The approach builds ultra-low energy buildings to reduce building energy use and related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same building service (comfort/lighting) → ↓ energy input (kWh/therms) → ↓ fuel combustion & upstream energy emissions → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: New buildings are designed/constructed to very high efficiency (envelope, insulation, airtightness, high-performance windows, passive design, efficient HVAC/lighting/appliances, commissioning).  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced thermal losses/gains and more efficient conversion of energy to end-use services lowers site and source energy consumption over the building lifetime.  
(3) Outcome: Lower energy demand leads to reduced operational GHG emissions (and often reduced peak load), with magnitude dependent on grid/fuel carbon intensity.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is lowering building energy demand, which typically reduces fossil-fuel energy use and associated GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: improved building design/technology reduces energy needed for the same services (efficiency) → downstream emissions reductions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while the ultimate benefit is mitigation, the mechanism’s defining first-order effect is reduced energy input (resource/energy efficiency), which applies regardless of the energy supply’s carbon content.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a policy explicitly requires on-site renewables or fuel switching as the main lever, that would shift the dominant pathway toward Mitigation via clean energy supply; however, “ultra-low energy buildings” is primarily demand reduction.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy implementing ultra-low-energy new buildings necessarily targets reduced operational energy consumption as the primary effect.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., passive design vs. advanced HVAC vs. envelope measures) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “ultra-low energy” is defined as net-zero through large on-site renewables rather than efficiency, or primarily through low-carbon energy supply, the dominant pathway could be Mitigation instead of Resource Efficiency; confirm wording/requirements in specific policies.
co_benefits: Lower energy bills; improved indoor comfort/health; reduced peak electricity demand
instance_edge_case_notes: Could alternatively map to a performance-standard or regulatory-mandate if the full policy text specifies building code requirements/EUI targets; here it is framed only as an “Approach,” so classified as planning/strategy.
